next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 15

By eleven the apartment had gone quiet in the careful way it did now, not peaceful, just arranged around not touching the sore parts. Sarah had gone to bed with a stack of essays and one of her red pens clipped into the neck of her T-shirt. Nate sat alone at the kitchen table under the yellow overhead light with Dominic's checkbook open, the latest bank statement flattened under his palm, and the December rent coupon pulled from under the fruit bowl.

Checking: twenty-two hundred.

Savings: eight hundred.

Wallet: forty-three dollars and a gas receipt.

Three thousand dollars was not money. Three thousand dollars was a delay. It was rent, the car, groceries, a little breathing room if Victor decided the weirdness had become more trouble than Dominic was worth. Without the job there was no backup plan hiding behind the math. There was Dominic's apartment, Dominic's Honda, Dominic's half-broken relationship, Dominic's name on the badge that got him into the office where his father's death had been filed into manila folders and signed for.

He had written the numbers twice already on the back of an electric bill, like rewriting them could make them expand.

The intercom buzzed hard enough to rattle the cheap metal frame by the stove.

He looked at the clock on the microwave. 11:07.

The buzz came again. Then Tommy's voice, flattened by static and hallway echo.

"Dom. C'mon."

Nate pressed the talk button. "What happened?"

"Nothing. Open the door."

He buzzed him in anyway.

From the bedroom Sarah made a small sound, not awake all the way, just registering disrup...
open_hooks_to_carry: Nate's better-prepared pitch: revised demolition plan, partial preservation, integration of existing businesses
Victor listening seriously — a different quality of attention than in Chapter 9
prev_tail_excerpt:
...n the wheel. Dominic's right hand, broader than his own had ever been, rested at ten and two like he was taking a driving lesson. These were the hands that had signed the notification letter. They were also the hands that had just accepted change from Mort Lazar and then promised Mort Lazar help.

He imagined going into the office Monday and opening the Eastbridge file again, not as a private act of penance this time but because he had said he would. He imagined Victor's voice, patient, well-fed, describing capital and timelines and everyone being cogs. He imagined trying to extract something useful from a machine designed specifically to transform useful people into paperwork. He imagined telling Mort, eventually, There's nothing, I checked, and seeing the exact moment that sentence turned him from neighbor into one more face on the wrong side.

A woman in a quilted vest crossed in front of his car carrying a takeout bag from Gail's. She glanced at him, saw nothing worth noting, and kept moving. The normality of that almost made him laugh.

He started the engine.

On the drive back to the apartment, the level clicked softly every time he braked. At a red light he reached over and set a hand on it to stop the noise. The aluminum was warming slowly from the car's heater and from the residual heat of his palm. He kept it there for the rest of the trip, as if it might slide away otherwise, as if letting go of the stupid thing would mean admitting that both of them, the tool and the promise, had been useless from the minute he picked them up and carried them toward the counter.
state_deltas_required: Victor's institutional power and the depth of political corruption behind the demolition — the machine that Dominic tried to fight and that Nate cannot dismantle

Nate understands the system Dominic was trapped in. The demolition is not one man's decision but a web of money, politics, and commitments that preceded Dominic and will outlast Nate. Victor's self-description as a cog reframes the entire moral landscape.
